unit.name: RNN sequence-to-sequence translation and the fixed-context bottleneck
unit.description: Introduces attention and transformers through the historical starting point of encoder-decoder RNNs for machine translation, then motivates why a single context vector becomes a bottleneck.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by positioning attention as a new primitive on sets of vectors and transformers as the dominant modern architecture across deep learning tasks [ts=78s][ts=93s]. It then rolls back historically to sequence-to-sequence translation with encoder-decoder RNNs: an encoder processes an input sentence token by token, and the final hidden state serves as a context vector summarizing the input for a decoder that generates the output sequence one word at a time [ts=239s][ts=317s][ts=382s]. The key limitation is that all communication from input to output flows through one fixed-length vector c, so longer sequences create an information bottleneck that becomes increasingly implausible for paragraphs, books, or larger corpora [ts=435s][ts=458s][ts=484s].

key_points.text: Attention is introduced as a neural-network primitive that operates on sets of vectors.
Transformers are introduced as architectures with self-attention at their core and described as the default large-model architecture today.
The encoder summarizes the entire input sequence into a single context vector, typically the last hidden state.
Decoder recurrence uses the previous output token, previous hidden state, and context vector to generate the output sequence.
A single fixed-size context vector creates a communication bottleneck between input and output sequences.

kp_catalog.name: Seq2seq bottleneck motivation for attention
kp_catalog.description: Encoder-decoder RNNs compress the input sequence into one context vector, which limits performance on longer sequences because all input-output communication must pass through that fixed-size representation. A single encoder hidden state is too narrow a bottleneck for long source sentences, motivating selective access to all encoder states during decoding.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because seq2seq bottleneck motivation for attention clarifies encoder-decoder RNNs compress the input sequence into one context vector, which limits performance on longer sequences because all input-output communication must pass through that fixed-size representation, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
